planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Perkins Mammoth Long Pod is an 1890s heirloom okra that produces very long (up to 8–10 inch), slightly ridged, green pods with outstanding flavor and a notably long harvest window before pods become tough — a longtime favorite in Southern and Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is warm; soak seeds overnight to improve germination. Perkins Mammoth Long Pod is an heirloom okra variety introduced in the 1890s that produces exceptionally long (6–10 inch) ribbed green pods with a more tender quality at larger sizes than most okra varieties — meaning the pods remain acceptable for eating somewhat longer than standard varieties, which quickly become fibrous and tough once they exceed 3–4 inches. The plants are vigorous (5–8 feet), highly productive, and heat-tolerant, performing well throughout the Gulf Coast and Deep South. Harvest pods at 4–6 inches for optimal tenderness; though the pods can be eaten somewhat larger, regular harvesting every 1–2 days in hot weather is essential to keep production high — missed pods left on the plant stop production quickly.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Star of David is an Israeli heirloom okra producing fat, round, six-ridged pods that look like a Star of David in cross-section, with a distinctive flavor and substantially larger pod diameter than standard American okra varieties — excellent for stuffing whole.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; soak seeds 12–24 hours for faster, more uniform germination. Star of David okra (also called Israeli okra or Giant Israeli okra) is a Middle Eastern heirloom producing fat, broadly triangular, six-ridged pods that create a hexagonal or Star-of-David shape in cross-section; the pods are substantially wider in diameter (1.5–2 inches) than the slender American varieties, making them excellent for stuffing whole (halved lengthwise and filled with seasoned meat and rice in the Middle Eastern manner, or stuffed and baked with tomato sauce). The flavor is mild and tender at 3–4 inch harvest size; the large pod diameter keeps them from becoming stringy at slightly larger sizes than standard okra. Plants are very tall (6–9 feet), vigorous, and ornamental with large yellow hibiscus flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Red Velvet is an ornamental-edible okra with stunning deep burgundy-red to velvet-red pods, stems, and veins that make it one of the most visually striking plants in the summer vegetable garden while producing tender, flavorful pods identical in taste to standard green okra.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Red Velvet is well-suited to the edible landscape as both a productive vegetable and ornamental plant with dramatic color. Red Velvet okra is one of the most ornamentally striking of all vegetable plants, producing intensely pigmented deep burgundy-red to crimson-velvet pods, stems, leaf veins, and even young leaves that make it outstanding as an edible ornamental in landscape plantings and containers; the yellow hibiscus flowers with purple-red centers add additional ornamental value. The pods are edible and flavorful at 3–4 inches, with a taste and texture identical to standard green okra; when cooked they unfortunately lose much of their dramatic red coloring (it leaches into the cooking water, which turns bright pink — interesting for certain preparations like red okra pickles). Plants are more compact than many okra varieties (4–6 feet), making them practical for smaller gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; Alabama Red is a tall, productive Deep South heirloom okra with dark red pods developed and maintained in Alabama home gardens, one of several distinct regional red okra heirloom strains and generally taller and more vigorous than shorter red okra types.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 18–24 inches in rows 3 feet apart; soak seeds overnight before planting. Alabama Red is a regional heirloom okra from Alabama home gardens, one of the distinct Deep South red-podded okra strains that differs from Red Velvet in being taller, more vigorous, and producing somewhat more slender pods with an intensely dark red to mahogany coloring; the flavor is excellent, with a slightly richer taste than some green varieties, and the pods are harvested at 3–4 inches for peak tenderness. Like other red okras, the pigment is water-soluble and leaches from the pods during cooking unless they are quickly roasted or fried. The tall plants (5–8 feet) with deeply lobed leaves and large yellow-centered flowers make Alabama Red an outstanding ornamental-edible for the Southern food garden. Maintained by seed savers in Alabama and nearby states and representative of the regional diversity in Southern okra cultivation.

common_name: Ronde de Nice Zucchini (Round French Zucchini)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-amended soil; Ronde de Nice is a classic French Provençal heirloom round zucchini, the traditional squash for stuffing in the Niçoise manner — the small, round fruits are hollowed out, filled with herbed meat or rice, and baked or braised in tomato sauce in what is arguably the definitive French stuffed squash preparation.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in well-amended, well-drained soil after last frost in full sun; Ronde de Nice is a bush-type that needs 2–3 feet of space and no support. Ronde de Nice is a French heirloom round summer squash of Provençal origin, the classic stuffing squash of Niçoise cuisine (courgettes rondes farcies à la niçoise) and one of the oldest and most culinarily significant French kitchen garden varieties still in regular cultivation. The fruits are perfectly spherical, 2–4 inches in diameter, with smooth, thin, pale to medium green skin and tender, mild, cream-colored flesh that has a somewhat denser texture than elongated zucchini — well-suited to stuffing as it holds its shape better during cooking. Harvest at 2–3 inches for fresh eating and 3–4 inches for stuffing; slightly larger fruits (4–5 inches) can still be stuffed but become seedy. Highly productive and heat-tolerant; excellent for Gulf Coast spring and fall production.
